skillName: Angles around a point sum to 360°
misconceptionSummary: Students do not connect the 360° rule to the idea of a full rotation and treat it as an arbitrary fact to memorise.

visuals.ruleText: A full rotation returns to the starting direction — that rotation is defined as 360°
visuals.subText: Any angles that together fill all directions from a point must sum to 360°

steps.narration: One complete rotation — turning all the way around until facing the original direction — is defined as three hundred and sixty degrees. Any set of angles that together cover all directions from a point are simply dividing up that one full rotation.

lines.text: Split into any regions — they share the 360°

steps.narration: Four right angles arranged around a point each claim ninety degrees of the full rotation — four times ninety is three hundred and sixty. Any other split — two angles, five angles, unequal angles — still totals three hundred and sixty, because they always share the same full rotation.

lines.text: Compass: N, E, S, W — four directions
From N back to N = full turn = 360°

steps.narration: Think of a compass: turning from north through east, south, west, and back to north is a full turn of three hundred and sixty degrees. Each quarter step is ninety degrees. The angles around any point work the same way — they divide a full turn.

visuals.expression: Full rotation = 360° — angles around a point share this rotation
visuals.label: The 360° rule is not arbitrary — it describes one complete rotation

steps.narration: Three hundred and sixty degrees — one complete rotation. Angles around a point are not a rule to memorise; they are simply the portions of one full rotation, which always total three hundred and sixty.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating 360° as an arbitrary number. It is not — it is the definition of one full rotation. Any angles that together complete a full turn from a single point must sum to 360°.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Three hundred and sixty degrees is a full rotation — turning all the way around. Angles around a point divide up that full rotation, so they must always total 360°. The rule describes a physical reality, not an arbitrary fact.
